data.title: LIV Golf: CEO Scott O’Neil bullish about future, says league ‘pushing the envelope’
data.description:
LIV Golf boss Scott O’Neil said his organisation was “pushing the envelope” as fast as it could in a bullish state of the union-style address at the end of the league’s media week in Florida.
In an upbeat assessment of the tour’s future, O’Neil said he was excited for what was coming, a day after Jon Rahm, Bryson DeChambeau and Cam Smith confirmed they had no interest in following Brooks Koepka back to the PGA Tour.
Speaking in a press conference alongside several senior executives, O’Neil expressed shock at the progress LIV had made in his first year in charge, which he said was a “testament to the team”.
“LIV is clearly at the top of the pyramid of golf outside the US. We are the world’s golf league,” O’Neil said.
“We are excited about what’s coming, and we are pushing the edge and the envelope as far and as fast as we can, and it’s great to work here.”

And while the relationship with the PGA is far from perfect, O’Neil said he had been welcomed with “open arms” by the likes of Mark Darbon and Mike Whan, his counterparts at the R&A and United States Golf Association, respectively.
Heading into a fifth season with increased prize money, a change to 72 holes and a hope that greater risk of relegation will bring world ranking points, the LIV boss said he thought the league was on the verge of bigger and better things.
“I feel like everywhere we turn, there are more and more people that want to see some structure and framework emerge in global golf, and I think we’re on the precipice of that,” he said.
The Asian Tour may find itself at the centre of that change, with O’Neil revealing plans for a “remaking and reshaping” of the International Series, the 10 elevated events that provide a pathway to LIV for the Tour’s members.
Established in 2022 with an initial US$300 million cash injection from what was LIV Golf Investments, the series has grown to include tournaments around the world, with the Link Hong Kong Open being one of them.
This year, the Fanling event was one of six around the world given Masters and Open Championship qualifying spots. Tom McKibbin won November’s tournament with a record score of 27-under-par.
According to O’Neil, the next iteration of the series will involve national opens and working with “some of the gifted and talented people at the golf federations around the world”.
Last year, the Asian Tour included the New Zealand, Korea, Singapore, Indonesia and Saudi Arabia opens on its schedule, and officials are believed to be working on adding others to that list.
LIV has stops in Australia, Spain and South Africa this season, while sources suggested some opens in Europe that have been relegated to second and third tier status on other tours could be involved.
“You’ll likely see four or five or six coming in this year, but if you fast forward out two, three years, you’ll have 10 to 12 of the biggest national opens will become the International Series,” O’Neil said.
“The reason is because we believe that national opens will drive more fans, drive more viewership and drive more people to this great game.”

data.title: US charges 20 people with rigging university, Chinese basketball games, NBC reports
data.description:
US authorities have charged 20 people ‌with rigging college basketball and Chinese Basketball Association ‍games, according to a federal indictment released on Thursday, including some individuals previously charged in an NBA-related gambling investigation.
The charges were unsealed ⁠by federal prosecutors in Philadelphia following a grand jury and accuse the defendants of conspiring on games from September 2022 until February 2025.
During the 2022-2023 CBA season, the ‍individuals sought to “fix” Chinese Basketball Association men’s basketball games by “point ‍shaving”, prosecutors alleged in the documents filed in the US District Court for the Eastern ‌District of Pennsylvania.
Point shaving is a form of cheating in ‍which individuals try to manipulate the margin of victory.
Some of those charged also allegedly sought to target games at Nicholls State University, Tulane University, Northwestern ‌State ⁠University, La Salle University, DePaul University, Robert Morris University, University of Southern Mississippi and North Carolina A&T State University.
Players at Kennesaw State University, Coppin State University, University of New ‌Orleans, Abilene Christian University, Eastern Michigan and Alabama State University were also targeted as part of the ‌scheme, according to ‌the indictment, which was reported earlier by NBC News.
In the 70-page indictment, authorities say the fixers recruited the basketball players with “bribe payments” usually ranging from US$10,000 to US$30,000 per game.
Concerns about gambling and university sports have grown since 2018, when the US Supreme Court struck down a federal ban on the practice, leading some states to legalise it to varying degrees.
The NCAA does not allow athletes or staff to bet on university games but it briefly allowed student-athletes to bet on professional sports last year before rescinding that decision in November.
More broadly, one betting scandal after another has rocked the sports world, where gambling revenue topped US$11 billion for the first three-quarters of last year, according to the American Gaming Association. That is up more than 13 per cent from the year before, the group said.
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Trump administration wins appeal to deport Mahmoud Khalil, a freed pro-Palestinian activist
data.description:
A federal appeal panel on Thursday reversed a lower court decision that released Mahmoud Khalil from an immigration jail, bringing the government one step closer to detaining and ultimately deporting the Palestinian activist.
A three-judge panel of the 3rd US Circuit Court of Appeal in Philadelphia instructed the lower court to dismiss Khalil’s habeas petition, a court filing that secured his release. The panel ruled that the federal district court in New Jersey did not have jurisdiction over the matter because immigration challenges are handled differently under the law.
In a 2-1 decision, the panel ruled that federal immigration laws require deportation challenges to be made by filing a petition for review of a final order of removal with a federal appeal court – not a lower level district court.
“That scheme ensures that petitioners get just one bite at the apple – not zero or two,” the panel wrote. “But it also means that some petitioners, like Khalil, will have to wait to seek relief for allegedly unlawful government conduct.”
The law bars Khalil “from attacking his detention and removal in a habeas petition,” the panel added.
Messages sent to Khalil and his legal team were not immediately returned.

The decision marked a major win for the Trump administration’s sweeping campaign to detain and deport non-citizens who joined protests against Israel.
It is likely not the final word in Khalil’s multipronged legal battle. Lawyers for the 30-year-old Palestinian activist have said they will exhaust all appeal options.
An outspoken leader of the pro-Palestinian movement at Columbia, Khalil was arrested at his flat on March 8, 2025. He then spent three months detained in a Louisiana immigration jail, missing the birth of his firstborn son.
Federal officials have accused Khalil of leading activities “aligned to Hamas”, though they have not presented evidence to back up the claim and have not accused him of criminal conduct.
The government has justified the arrest under a seldom-used statute that allows for the expulsion of non-citizens whose beliefs are deemed to pose a threat to US foreign policy interests.
In June, a federal judge in New Jersey ruled that justification would likely be declared unconstitutional and ordered Khalil released.

US President Donald Trump’s administration then appealed that ruling, arguing the deportation decision should ultimately fall to an immigration judge, rather than a federal court. They have also accused Khalil of failing to disclose information on his green card application.
Khalil has dismissed those allegations as “baseless and ridiculous”, framing his arrest and detention as a “direct consequence of exercising my right to free speech as I advocated for a free Palestine and an end to the genocide in Gaza”.
The appeal court decision comes as an appeals board in the immigration court system is weighing a previous order from an immigration judge that found Khalil could be deported. His lawyers have argued that the federal order should take precedence.
That judge has suggested that Khalil could be deported to Algeria, where he maintains citizenship through a distant relative, or Syria, where he was born in a refugee camp to a Palestinian family.
His lawyers have said he would face mortal danger if he were forced to return to either country.

data.title: ‘Hong Kong lawyers should not aim just to compete but specialise in Greater Bay Area’
data.description:
Hong Kong lawyers should not think of competing head-on with mainland counterparts in the Greater Bay Area, but instead position themselves as common law specialists and collaborators in cross-border legal teams, Hong Kong Bar Association chairman Jose-Antonio Maurellet has said.
His comments came during a fireside chat on the sidelines of the China Conference: Greater Bay Area event hosted by the South China Morning Post in Shenzhen on Thursday.
Recent policy measures allow Hong Kong lawyers to qualify for practice in parts of mainland China. In 2020, Beijing authorities introduced an exam pathway enabling solicitors and barristers to handle certain cases – including commercial matters – in nine Greater Bay Area mainland cities.
Maurellet described the arrangement as a positive development, but cautioned against viewing it as a route for Hong Kong lawyers to compete directly with mainland practitioners across the board, even if they pass the exam.
“We’re certainly not going to flood the mainland market because it’s very competitive,” he said. “In terms of the market perception between you and someone who has actually spent five years learning about mainland Chinese law, why would a client use you over that person, who really, has spent all of his career and studies just under that system?”
Maurellet said Hong Kong lawyers were more likely to contribute as part of joint legal teams in cases with both mainland and common law elements.
They could add value in disputes involving Hong Kong law, common law interpretation or international commercial practice, he added.
He compared the arrangement to international arbitration, where legal teams often include lawyers from different jurisdictions, each handling issues within their area of expertise.
In cases governed by foreign law, specialists in that legal system typically take the lead, while others defer, he said.
“It actually gives businessmen a lot of choice,” Maurellet said. “Different systems have different strengths and that diversity can be used to your advantage.”
He said the scheme could support longer-term cooperation between Hong Kong and mainland legal professionals, as lawyers who had worked together on Greater Bay Area matters were more likely to collaborate when disputes were heard in Hong Kong or resolved through international arbitration.
Such professional links could reinforce Hong Kong’s position as a regional dispute resolution centre, particularly in arbitration, where parties are free to choose both venue and legal representation.

Maurellet drew comparisons with the Singapore International Commercial Court, where foreign lawyers can appear in certain cases but typically work with mixed teams rather than dominating proceedings.
Similar market dynamics applied in other mature legal markets, he said.
Hong Kong’s role, he added, continued to be shaped by its common law system, international legal environment and experience in handling complex cross-border disputes.
Asked about the high-profile case of former media boss Jimmy Lai Chee-ying, who recently returned to court for mitigation hearings following his conviction under the national security law, Maurellet said the broader concern was the perception of Hong Kong abroad being forged that did not match the reality on the ground.
“I won’t talk about any specific cases, especially those which are ongoing, but generally speaking, we shouldn’t avoid the elephants in the room,” he said.
He acknowledged that certain cases were taking up “a lot of airspace and newspaper columns” locally and abroad, creating a misperception among some that Hong Kong was not safe.
“What is important, for us in the private sector in particular, is to share what I like to call the true Hong Kong story,” he said.
“It’s not going about saying everything is wonderful, but to say, ‘Look these things are like this, these things are like that’, and we have to give a holistic picture.”
He also discussed how companies in Zhuhai and the wider Greater Bay Area could make better use of Hong Kong’s legal expertise, advising on complex contracts and cross-border transactions.
Collaborative teams combining common law and mainland expertise were key to lowering risk and maximising outcomes, he added.
On talent, Maurellet said Hong Kong lawyers were highly adaptable and multilingual.
“People of my generation … our levels of Mandarin were passable,” he said. “If you talk to somebody who’s 25 joining my chambers today, they’re very fluent with a lot of native-level speaking people … there’s actually a lot of talent both inside and outside Hong Kong and people are astute – when they see opportunities they adapt.”
He said the region’s diversity of legal systems could be used strategically, noting that each system has its own strengths and limitations.
Hong Kong’s legal framework, he said, was well suited for larger, more complex disputes, while smaller cases may not always justify the same level of resources.
Businesses, he added, tended to choose the system that best fit their needs, allowing them to leverage the variety of legal options available across the Greater Bay Area.

data.title: Trump threatens to use Insurrection Act to end protests in Minneapolis
data.description:
US President Donald Trump on Thursday threatened to invoke the Insurrection Act and deploy troops to quell persistent protests against the federal officers sent to Minneapolis to enforce his administration’s massive immigration crackdown.
The president’s threat comes a day after a federal immigration officer shot and wounded a Minneapolis man who had attacked the officer with a shovel and broom handle. That shooting further heightened the fear and anger radiating across the Minnesota city since an Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent fatally shot a Renee Good in the head.
Trump has repeatedly threatened to invoke the rarely used federal law to deploy the US military or federalise the National Guard for domestic law enforcement, over the objections of state governors.
“If the corrupt politicians of Minnesota don’t obey the law and stop the professional agitators and insurrectionists from attacking the Patriots of I.C.E., who are only trying to do their job, I will institute the INSURRECTION ACT, which many Presidents have done before me, and quickly put an end to the travesty that is taking place in that once great State,” Trump said in a social media post.
Requests for comment have been sent to the offices of Governor Tim Walz and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey.
The Department of Homeland Security says it has made more than 2,000 arrests in the state since early December and is vowing not to back down. ICE is an agency under the United States Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

Protests, tear gas and another shooting
In Minneapolis, smoke filled the streets on Wednesday night near the site of the latest shooting as federal officers wearing gas masks and helmets fired tear gas into a small crowd. Protesters responded by throwing rocks and shooting fireworks.
Police Chief Brian O’Hara said during a news conference that the gathering was an unlawful assembly and “people need to leave”.
Things later quietened down and by early on Thursday only a few demonstrators and law enforcement officers remained at the scene.
Demonstrations have become common on the streets of Minneapolis since the ICE agent fatally shot 37-year-old Good on January 7. Agents have yanked people from their cars and homes, and have been confronted by angry bystanders demanding that the officers pack up and leave.
“This is an impossible situation that our city is presently being put in and at the same time we are trying to find a way forward to keep people safe, to protect our neighbours, to maintain order,” Frey, the mayor, said.
Frey said the federal force – five times the size of the city’s 600-officer police force – has “invaded” Minneapolis, scaring and angering residents.

Shooting followed a chase
In a statement describing the events that led to Wednesday’s shooting, Homeland Security said federal law enforcement officers stopped a driver from Venezuela who is in the US illegally. The person drove away and crashed into a parked car before taking off on foot, DHS said.
After officers reached the person, two other people arrived from a nearby flat and all three started attacking the officer, according to DHS.
“Fearing for his life and safety as he was being ambushed by three individuals, the officer fired a defensive shot to defend his life,” DHS said.
The two people who came out of the flat are in custody, it said.
O’Hara said the man shot was in hospital with a non life-threatening injury.
The shooting took place about 7km (4.5 miles) north of where Good was killed. O’Hara’s account of what happened largely echoed that of Homeland Security.
During a speech before the latest shooting, Walz described Minnesota as being in chaos, saying what’s happening in the state “defies belief”.
“Let’s be very, very clear, this long ago stopped being a matter of immigration enforcement,” he said. “Instead, it’s a campaign of organised brutality against the people of Minnesota by our own federal government.”

Official says agent who killed Good was injured
Jonathan Ross, the US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer who killed Good, suffered internal bleeding to his torso during the encounter, a Homeland Security official said.
The official spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss Ross’ medical condition. The official did not provide details about the severity of the injuries, and the agency did not respond to questions about the extent of the bleeding, exactly how he suffered the injury, when it was diagnosed or his medical treatment.
Good was killed after three ICE officers surrounded her SUV on a snowy street a few blocks from her home.
Bystander video shows one officer ordering Good to open the door and grabbing the handle. As the vehicle begins to move forward, Ross, standing in front, raises his weapon and fires at least three shots at close range. He steps back as the SUV advances and turns.
Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem has said Ross was struck by the vehicle and that Good was using her SUV as a weapon – a self-defence claim that has been criticised by Minnesota officials.
Chris Madel, a lawyer for Ross, declined to comment.
Good’s family has hired the same law firm that represented George Floyd’s family in a US$27 million settlement with Minneapolis. Floyd, who was Black, died after a white police officer pinned his neck to the ground in the street in May 2020.

data.title: Bid-rigging and loopholes: pledges fall short of fixing Hong Kong’s building sector
data.description:
New measures to strengthen Hong Kong’s building maintenance regime after the deadly Tai Po fire only scratch the surface of long-standing issues that require deeper systemic reforms and tougher laws, according to experts and industry figures.
The damning verdict came after the government’s pledges and proposals on Wednesday to close policy gaps and tackle entrenched problems laid bare by the blaze at Wang Fuk Court.
At the first Legislative Council meeting of the year, officials unveiled a raft of proposals, including a new requirement for service providers seeking contracts to undergo background checks by the police and the Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC), as well as criminal and disciplinary record screening to qualify for a government platform.
The inferno broke out in November during renovation works at Wang Fuk Court, a subsidised housing estate in Tai Po, claiming 168 lives – the city’s deadliest fire in seven decades.
While describing the measures as a step in the right direction, industry leaders warned that “the devil is in the detail”, as the government’s proposals currently exist only as a framework lacking specific content.
“The proposed screening process must specify which criminal and disciplinary records are to be considered, and whether professional misconduct that stops short of a criminal offence will be included,” lawmaker and town planner Andrew Lam Siu-lo said.
He questioned whether the vetting would apply only to company directors or extend to Registered Authorised Persons (APs), warning that focusing solely on the former could create a loophole. This, he said, could allow firms to continue employing APs with poor track records, including those suspected of involvement in bid-rigging.
Another proposal by authorities seeks to expand the role of the Urban Renewal Authority (URA) in private building maintenance and amending the Buildings Ordinance by upgrading large-scale maintenance projects under the Minor Works Control System. Homeowners would also be required to hire independent consultants to oversee major works.
“Under the current regulations, it is not a must for registered professionals to conduct on-site inspections during construction. How effective would it be to hire a third-party consultant if they are not required to be physically present all of the time to oversee the works?” questioned Terence Mang Ka-wing, immediate past president of the Hong Kong Institute of Construction Managers.
He said the government’s initiative was well-intentioned but stressed that reforms must be comprehensive. Mang suggested establishing a transparent platform to help flat owners understand industry pricing and the background of construction firms, which would enable them to make informed choices.

Vincent Ho Kui-yip, president of the Hong Kong Institute of Building Safety, said the URA’s expanded role in private maintenance would demand far greater resources to be effective.
“It is also a must to actively track and disclose the long-term track records and hidden networks of contractors, which is a level of oversight that should not rest solely on a statutory body,” he said.
Ho proposed creating an independent team to monitor contractor networks and maintain an open, transparent database, which would allow residents to access crucial information before choosing whom to hire.
In the case of Wang Fuk Court, the contractor involved – Prestige Construction & Engineering Co Limited – reportedly received a perfect “project management score” of 100 under the URA’s Building Rehabilitation Company Registration Scheme.
The URA later admitted that the existing scoring format could mislead the public and that the scheme currently does not require contractors to declare criminal convictions related to site safety. It pledged to review and refine the registration list.
Meanwhile, the blaze laid bare an open secret in the murky world of building renovation – a sector plagued by bid-rigging and soaring costs.
Internal documents revealed that the renovation budget for Wang Fuk Court ballooned from HK$152 million (US$19.5 million) in September 2023 to HK$336 million last year after the estate opted for the most expensive tender.
One common malpractice involves consulting firms bidding low to secure contracts, then collaborating with affiliated contractors and owners’ corporations to inflate project prices well above actual costs.
To tackle the issue, the government also proposed revising the homeowners’ voting mechanism by raising the threshold for the proportion of owners required to be physically present to vote on major maintenance projects. It also plans to cap the number of proxy instruments any individual can hold.
Some Wang Fuk Court residents previously complained about “a sudden surge in proxy votes” during an election, saying this made it difficult for individual homeowners to challenge owners’ corporation decisions.
Thomas Cheng, a competition law expert and professor at the University of Hong Kong, said tightening proxy voting rules was a positive step, but a genuine crackdown on bid-rigging would require further legislative changes, including criminalising the practice, accompanied by a visible commitment to enforcement and a dedicated allocation of resources.
He said that current penalties for bid-rigging under the Competition Ordinance amount only to fines – negligible compared to the vast profits generated by these schemes – providing little deterrent effect.

data.title: Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon: North Korean quintet among invited runners
data.description:
The organisers of the Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon have invited two of the world’s top 100 runners and a group of North Koreans to be part of this year’s race.
Of the 26 men and 19 women included in the elite category, Kenya and Ethiopia have sent 37 athletes to compete, while there is also an Asian Games silver medallist in the mix.
The fastest man in the field is Ethiopia’s Mekuant Ayenew, who is ranked 98th in the world and has a personal best time of 2 hours, four minutes and 46 seconds. He will wear bib No 1 on Sunday.
Bahrain’s Shitaye Eshete is the quickest woman, with a fastest time of 2:20:32, which she clocked at last year’s Haspa Marathon Hamburg.
The elite list also features one man and four women from North Korea, including Asian champion and North Korea’s national record holder Han Il-ryong.

Han, who drew attention at the 2022 Hangzhou Asian Games by winning a silver medal without using top-end carbon shoes or energy gels, ran his personal best of 2:09:42 at the 2024 Wuxi Marathon and will wear bib No 19.
Among the North Korean women, the fastest is the 22-year-old Jon Su-gyong, with a best of 2:25:48.
This is not the first time North Korean runners have raced in the city, with athletes from the secretive state enjoying a storied history at the marathon.
Kim Hye-gyong claimed the women’s title in 2:31:46 in 2015, while in 2008 Kim Kum-ok and Jong Yong-ok memorably crossed the finish line hand in hand, with the then 19-year-old Kim taking the crown.
She later finished fourth in 2012 and third in 2013 while also winning the Asian Championships.
Japan’s famed Yuki Kawauchi will also return to the city after finishing seventh in last year’s Bank of China Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge Half Marathon, in what will be his first invited appearance at the Standard Chartered-backed event.
A former civil servant, Kawauchi gained fame by winning the 2018 Boston Marathon as an amateur runner, earning the title of “The Citizen Runner”.

Kawauchi was a hit with local runners on his last Hong Kong visit, being mobbed for autographs and selfies after the race.
Taking place this Sunday, the city’s largest marathon event expects to draw 74,000 runners across the full marathon, half-marathon, 10km and wheelchair 10km races, with 25 per cent of them from mainland China or overseas – the highest ever.
Those runners come from 110 countries and regions, following a total of nearly 120,000 record sign-ups for the entry lottery.
This year’s race offers a total prize pot of US$314,800, with the men’s and women’s full marathon champions each earning US$65,000. An additional US$12,000 is on offer for any athlete who breaks the course record.

data.title: European allies send troops to Greenland amid Trump’s purchase pressure
data.description:
European countries were sending small numbers of military personnel to Greenland on Thursday as Denmark and its allies ‌prepared for exercises to try to assure US President Donald Trump of its security as he pushes to acquire the island.
A meeting of officials from the United States, Denmark ‍and Greenland on Wednesday avoided the type of public humiliation meted out to Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky last year, but also produced no quick fix for the dispute.
“The American ambition to take over Greenland is intact,” Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen said in a written comment to Reuters on Thursday, describing a “fundamental disagreement”.
“That is of course serious, and therefore we continue our efforts to prevent this scenario becoming a reality.”
Trump cites Russian and Chinese interests
Trump has said the strategically located and mineral-rich island is ⁠vital to US security and that the US must own it to prevent Russia or China occupying it. He has said all options are on the table for securing the territory, which is an autonomous territory of Denmark.
He says Denmark cannot ward off Russian and Chinese influence in the Arctic region.
Russia said Nato’s talk of Moscow and Beijing being a threat to Greenland was a myth designed to whip up hysteria and warned of the dangers of escalating confrontation in the region.
There is currently little evidence that a large number of Chinese and Russian ships sail near Greenland’s coasts.
Greenland and Denmark say the island is not for sale, ‍that threats of force are reckless and security concerns should be resolved among allies.
Prominent EU countries have backed Denmark, warning a US military seizure of Greenland could in effect spell the end of Nato.
Before Wednesday’s meeting in ‍the US, Greenland and Denmark said they had begun to increase their military presence in and around Greenland in cooperation with Nato allies.
Germany, France, Sweden and Norway have said they are sending military staff to begin preparations for larger drills later this year.
“The Danish Armed Forces, together with a number of Arctic and European allies, will explore ‌in the coming weeks how an increased presence and exercise activity in the Arctic can be implemented in practice,” the Danish Ministry of Defence said.




Initial deployment appears small
Danish Foreign Minister Lars Lokke Rasmussen said on Wednesday about ‍200 US troops were stationed in Greenland, which has a population of around 57,000.
The scale of the planned European military build-up has not been made public, but initial deployments appear small.
The German Armed Forces were deploying a reconnaissance team of 13, first to Copenhagen, before heading on to Greenland with Danish personnel. Late on Wednesday, a Danish Air Force plane landed at Nuuk airport and personnel in military fatigues disembarked.
Sweden was sending three officers, and Norway two. Olivier Poivre d’Arvor, France’s ambassador to the Poles, said France was sending about 15 mountain specialists.
“A ‌first ⁠team of French military personnel is already on the ground and will be reinforced in the coming days by land, air and naval assets,” French President Emmanuel Macron said.
France and the European Union as a whole must be “unyielding in upholding territorial sovereignty”, he added.
One British officer was joining the reconnaissance group. The Netherlands said it was willing to send staff and would take a decision this week. Poland said it would not send soldiers.
The European military deployment to Greenland sends two messages to the US administration, said Marc Jacobsen, an associate professor at the Royal Danish Defence College.

“One is to deter, is to show that ‘if you ‌decide to do something militarily, we’re ready to defend Greenland’,” he said. “And the other purpose is to say: ‘Well, we take your critique seriously, we increase our presence, take care of our sovereignty, and improve surveillance over Greenland’.”
After meeting US Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Vice-President J.D. Vance on Wednesday, Rasmussen and Greenland’s foreign minister, Vivian Motzfeldt, said the US and Denmark would form ‌a working group to discuss concerns regarding the island.
Greenland’s Prime Minister Jens-Frederik Nielsen reiterated on Facebook on Thursday that the island did not want to be governed by, or owned by, the United States, and that it would remain part of Denmark and the Nato alliance.
“Now is not the time for internal discussions. Now is the time for unity, calm and responsibility. I’m following the situation closely, and I stand with you to look after Greenland,” Jens-Frederik Nielsen said.

data.title: The US has sent a ‘dangerous’ message in Venezuela and China must act, legal expert warns
data.description:
China needs to legislate to protect itself against the risk of “external suppression” and safeguard its technology and supply chains, a legal expert with a top Beijing think tank has warned.
Li Honglei, head of a Communist Party committee that oversees two law institutes under the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, said tighter national security legislation was needed to make the country more “resilient”.
He said the risks had been laid bare by America’s abduction of the former Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro and his wife on January 3.
That US military operation sent a “dangerous” message that Washington could increasingly try to bypass international law and enforce its own legal system on other countries, according to Li.
He made the remarks on Wednesday during a webinar on the US national security law framework held by Renmin University’s National Academy of Development and Strategy in Beijing.


Li also said the US National Security Strategy released in December – which highlights economic security – showed that Washington was moving from an “expansive” hegemony to a “defensive” position.
He said the US posture was also a reminder for Beijing that “resources must be concentrated in the fundamental areas that are crucial to our national destiny”.
Li said the country should avoid “overspending” strategically across a wide range of fields.
“[China’s] legal policies should fully safeguard technological self-reliance and supply chain security,” he said.
Li called for schemes to support basic research, core technologies and strategic material reserves that involved a solid legal grounding as well as long-term funding.
“It is not just about investment,” he said.
“More critically, it is about refining the legal system to create an institutional environment that encourages original innovation, protects intellectual property and streamlines industrial circulation.
“That is the bedrock for us to cope with all external suppression.”
It comes amid an intensifying strategic rivalry between China and the United States as the two powers vie for dominance in hi-tech sectors such as semiconductors and artificial intelligence.
Both countries are jostling for more control over the critical minerals and supply chains that power those advanced technologies.
Beijing has put more emphasis on hi-tech self-reliance and home-grown innovation, which was listed as a top priority in the proposed five-year plan to 2030 approved by the ruling Communist Party in October.
Washington, meanwhile, vowed in its latest national security document to “ensure that US technology and US standards – particularly in AI, biotech and quantum computing – drive the world forward” and to “control our own supply chains”.
At a gathering of finance ministers in Washington on Monday, Group of Seven countries “expressed a strong, shared desire to quickly address key vulnerabilities in critical minerals supply chains”, according to the US Department of the Treasury.
US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent showed “his optimism that nations will pursue prudent de-risking over decoupling” in the meeting, alluding to diversifying away from Chinese supplies.
Meanwhile, Greenland’s rich mineral wealth has been widely seen as one of the factors behind US President Donald Trump’s talk of taking over the semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, a Nato ally of the US.


Speaking during the webinar, Li said China should avoid falling into a “securitisation trap” that could hinder technological progress.
“Our national legislators and decision-makers need to establish finer risk assessment and scrutiny mechanisms to ensure that security measures are necessary, reasonable and proportionate,” he said.
He also called for the implementation rules of China’s Anti-Foreign Sanctions Law to be improved, and for tougher legislation in areas such as data and finance.
“These tools should ... create effective deterrence without undermining international cooperation networks,” Li said.
Addressing the same event, Yawei Liu, a senior adviser on China at The Carter Center, a non-profit organisation in Atlanta, noted that security was a pressing issue for both Beijing and Washington.
“Both countries are so large – economically, militarily and in terms of influence – that an action taken by either one that ignores the concerns of the other will create insecurity,” he said, adding that more face-to-face meetings between Chinese and US officials to discuss the issue were needed.

data.title: US seizes another Venezuela-linked tanker ahead of Trump-Machado meeting
data.description:
The United ‌States has seized another ‍Venezuela-linked tanker, two US officials told Reuters on Thursday, ⁠ahead of a meeting between US President Donald Trump and ‍Venezuelan opposition leader Maria Corina Machado.
The ‍seizure marks the sixth vessel ‌targeted in recent weeks that was ‍either carrying Venezuelan oil or had done so ‌in the past.
The ⁠officials, speaking on the condition of anonymity, said the seizure took ‌place in the Caribbean, but did ‌not ‌identify the vessel.
More to follow …
